---
title: 'The Entire Book of Job - For Further Study'
date: '2013-11-08T04:02:00.0000000+00:00'
draft: true
blogger_blog: 'Darrell Wolfe, Storyteller'
blogger_status: 'LIVE'
blogger_post_id: '7301141429059938782'
blogger_filename: '/2013/11/the-entire-book-of-job-for-further-study.html'
blogger_source: 'takeout-20260223T235336Z-3-001.zip:Takeout/Blogger/Blogs/Darrell Wolfe, Storyteller/feed.atom'
categories:
  - 'Job Research'
  - 'study'
  - 'Job'
---

<h2 style="text-align: center;"><a href="http://www.biblestudytools.com/nkjv/job/1.html" target="_blank">The Book of Job - New King James Version </a></h2><h3 style="text-align: left;">The following is mostly just a cut and paste of the book of Job, but re-ordering the material for further study, along with my notes in <i>Italics</i>.:</h3><h4>The Back Story</h4><i>There are some things that can help understanding of this book. The entire middle of this book is dedicated to speeches Job gives and his friends give to him and his responses to them. There are Key moments throughout that give the entire book light and meaning. </i><br /><br /><i><b>The Book of Job</b> takes place in a period of time between the time after Abraham and probably before Joseph. Job may have met Joseph after he became ruler of Egypt. Some believe that Job's second prosperity came under the time of Joseph's rule. No hard evidence to confirm or deny. Job is Joseph's Nephew. (Son of Issachar who is Joseph's Brother.)&nbsp;&nbsp;</i><br /><br /><i><b>Job</b>'s father is Issachar, whose father is Jacob/Israel (Job's  grandfather), whose  father is Issac (Job's Great Grandfather), whose   father is Abraham (Jobs Great Great Grandfather).<a href="http://www.biblestudytools.com/nkjv/genesis/46-13.html"> Genesis 46:13 NKJV. </a></i><br /><i>Job lives in <a href="http://www.biblestudytools.com/search/?q=uz" target="_blank">UZ, which is part of greater Edom</a>. <a href="http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Edom" target="_blank">Edom (Idumea) in the south of modern Israel</a>, it connects the <a href="http://goo.gl/maps/1Byfb" target="_blank">Dead Sea to the Gulf of Aqaba</a>. It also encompasses parts of modern south Jordan. </i><br /><i><br /></i><i>Given that Job's exact age is not listed. The only reference given is that after Job was restored he lived another <a href="http://www.biblestudytools.com/kjv/job/42.html" target="_blank">140 years</a> and saw his kids, and kids kids to the fourth generation. If he was the wealthiest man in the east it's reasonable to say he was probably old enough to have gathered that wealth, and he did have 7 sons and three daughters before the incident. So he was old enough to have had time for them to grow up and be partiers and have their own houses each. I'm thinking his kids were early twenties thirties, so he was probably 50-60 years old. Not old for his time, but no spring chicken either. </i><br /><i><br /></i><i>It's hard to read all the words Job speaks, and his friends speak about him, and not come to the conclusion that there really wasn't then, and may never have been since (except for Jesus) a man who lived a holy and righteous a life as Job. Yet comparing Job to Abraham we see a man who is trusting in his OWN righteousness to justify him, where as Abraham trusts only in God's righteousness. Which is why Abraham is the father of faith. </i><br /><br /><i><b>Eliphaz </b>is the son of Esau (<a href="http://www.biblestudytools.com/search/?q=eliphaz" target="_blank">Gen 36, 1 Chron 1:35</a>) also known as Edom.(<a href="http://www.biblestudytools.com/search/?q=esau+edom&amp;c=&amp;t=kjv&amp;ps=10&amp;s=Bibles" target="_blank">Gen 25, Gen 36,</a>) The people of Edom were known for their wisdom. <a href="http://www.biblestudytools.com/search/?q=esau+edom&amp;c=&amp;t=kjv&amp;ps=10&amp;s=Bibles" target="_blank"> Obadiah 1:8</a>. Eliphaz was a resident in the city of <a href="http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Teman_%28Edom%29" target="_blank">Teman</a> in the land of Edom. Therefore Eliphaz is the son of Job's grandfather's brother. (Jacob's Brother is Esau). <a href="http://www.biblestudytools.com/lexicons/hebrew/kjv/eliyphaz.html" target="_blank">Eliphaz </a>name means "My God is fine gold". </i><br /><i><br /></i><i><b>Bildad </b>the Shuhite is a descendant of <a href="http://www.bibler.org/versions/american_standard/asv_genesis25_sml.htm#genesis_25:1" target="_blank">Shuah </a>who is another son of Abraham through his third wife <a href="http://www.bibler.org/versions/american_standard/asv_genesis25_sml.htm#genesis_25:1" target="_blank">Keturah</a>. Not much else is known about him. He was most likely from Arabia which is where the descendants of Keturah lived. <a href="http://www.bibler.org/versions/american_standard/asv_genesis25_sml.htm#genesis_25:1" target="_blank">Genesis 25:6</a>, Abraham sent them to live east of Canaan away from Issac. Some say that Bildad's name means "Bel has Loved". <a href="http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bel_%28mythology%29" target="_blank">Bel </a>was a god name in ancient Babylon. <a href="http://www.biblestudytools.com/lexicons/hebrew/kjv/bildad.html" target="_blank">Strongs </a>says it means "Confusing (by mingling) Love". </i><br /><i><br /></i><i><b>Zophar </b>a resident of Naamah in Canaan. Zophar's name means simply "Sparrow". Little else is known and he speaks the least.&nbsp;</i><br /><i><br /></i><i><b>Elihu </b>is a <a href="http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Elihu_%28Job%29" target="_blank">Buzite</a>, may be a descendant of Abraham or a nephew of Abraham. Elihu's name means "He is my God". He acts in the role of intercessor. He is the one filled with the spirit of God and rebukes Job for his real problem, which is (Job 32) justifying himself rather than God. In fact the entire message of Job is about the fallen nature of Man and the redeemer Jesus who is to come. It's hard to read Elihu's speech without seeing Jesus written all over it.</i><br /><br /><i>Now I metioned Job is probably 50-60 here. His friends are more than likely the same age or older. Elihu says that they should speak because their age should have wisdom, so he is younger than them. I'm guessing he's either a teenager, or young twenties. I've noticed a special anointing on teens and young adults to see and speak the things of God. Most revivals start with this age group. So I'm guessing here, but he could be between 17-25. &nbsp; </i><br /><h4>The Opening Scene</h4><div style="text-align: center;"><b>&nbsp;Chapter One</b></div><span class="versetext" id="job1-1" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">1</span>    There was a man in the land of Uz, whose name was Job; <b>and that man  was blameless and upright</b>, and one who feared God and shunned evil.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job1-2" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">2</span>    And seven sons and three daughters were born to him.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job1-3" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">3</span>    Also, his possessions were seven thousand sheep, three thousand  camels, five hundred yoke of oxen, five hundred female donkeys, and a  very large household, so that this man was the greatest of all the  people of the East.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job1-4" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">4</span>    And his sons would go and feast in their houses, each on his  appointed day, and would send and invite their three sisters to eat and  drink with them.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job1-5" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">5</span>    So it was, when the days of feasting had run their course, that Job  would send and sanctify them, and he would rise early in the morning and  offer burnt offerings according to the number of them all. <u><b>For Job  said, "It may be that my sons have sinned and <span style="font-size: large;">cursed <a href="http://draft.blogger.com/null" name="a"></a> God</span> in their hearts." </b></u>Thus Job did regularly.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job1-6" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">6</span>    Now there was a day when the sons of God came to present themselves before the Lord, and Satan <a href="http://draft.blogger.com/null" name="b"></a> also came among them.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job1-7" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">7</span>    And the Lord said to Satan, "From where do you come?" So Satan  answered the Lord and said, "From going to and fro on the earth, and  from walking back and forth on it." <b>   </b></span><b>          <span class="versetext" id="job1-8" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">8</span>    Then the Lord said to Satan, "Have you considered My servant Job,  that there is none like him on the earth, a blameless and upright man,  one who fears God and shuns evil?"    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job1-9" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">9</span>    So Satan answered the Lord and said, "Does Job fear God for nothing?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job1-10" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">10</span>    Have You not made a hedge around him, around his household, and  around all that he has on every side? You have blessed the work of his  hands, and his possessions have increased in the land.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job1-11" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">11</span>    But now, stretch out Your hand and touch all that he has, <u>and he will surely <span style="font-size: large;">curse </span>You to Your face</u>!"    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job1-12" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">&nbsp;</span></span></b><br /><br /><br /><b><span class="versetext" id="job1-12" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">12</span>    And the Lord said to Satan, "Behold, all that he has<u> is in </u>your  power; only do not lay a hand on his person."&nbsp;</span></b>       <br /><br /><i>*Notice: God didn't say he was giving Satan power over Job, he was drawing Satan's attention to the fact he already had it...*</i><br /><br /><i>*Notice Also: Satan sets the theme of his attacks in this book. Satan wants to get Job to curse God and we'll see that as a theme throughout this book. Satan works every angle he can to poison Job's Mind to Curse God. That is the sin he is after, that's what he wants out of Job. He had already been working Job before this encounter with God, because we see Job has been engaging in fear based (not faith based) sacrifices, "in case they may have cursed God in their hearts". <a href="http://www.kcm.org/real-help/article/fear-or-faith-choice-yours" target="_blank">Fear attaches you to the Curse and Faith attaches you to The Blessing</a>. This fear based approach is what opened the hedge around Job's life before this conversation between God and Satan ever occurred. Why would God point it out? Because if it continued Job's eternity could have been at stake, he would have allowed fear so much control that he would have eventually cursed God and died. Which is nearly what he started to do as he get's into his speeches, by blaming God for attacking him unjustly. To call the God of Justice, unjust, is nearly cursing God. He came real close. *</i><br /><h4>The Attacks</h4><h4>&nbsp;Attack 1: Everything but his body...</h4>&nbsp;<span class="versetext" id="job1-12" style="display: inline;">So Satan went out from the  presence of the Lord.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job1-13" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">13</span>    Now there was a day when his sons and daughters were eating and drinking wine in their oldest brother's house;    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job1-14" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">14</span>    and a messenger came to Job and said, "The oxen were plowing and the donkeys feeding beside them,    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job1-15" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">15</span>    when the Sabeans <a href="http://draft.blogger.com/null" name="c"></a> raided them and took them  away--indeed they have killed the servants with the edge of the sword;  and I alone have escaped to tell you!"    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job1-16" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">16</span>    While he was still speaking, another also came and said, "The fire  of God fell from heaven and burned up the sheep and the servants, and  consumed them; and I alone have escaped to tell you!"    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job1-17" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">17</span>    While he was still speaking, another also came and said, "The  Chaldeans formed three bands, raided the camels and took them away, yes,  and killed the servants with the edge of the sword; and I alone have  escaped to tell you!"    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job1-18" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">18</span>    While he was still speaking, another also came and said, "Your sons  and daughters were eating and drinking wine in their oldest brother's  house,    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job1-19" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">19</span>    and suddenly a great wind came from across <a href="http://draft.blogger.com/null" name="d"></a> the  wilderness and struck the four corners of the house, and it fell on the  young people, and they are dead; and I alone have escaped to tell you!"    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job1-20" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">20</span>    Then Job arose, tore his robe, and shaved his head; and he fell to the ground and worshiped.    </span>          <b><span class="versetext" id="job1-21" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">21</span>    And he said:  "Naked I came from my mother's womb, And naked shall I  return there. The Lord gave, and the Lord has taken away; Blessed be  the name of the Lord."    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job1-22" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">22</span>    In all this Job did not sin nor charge God with wrong.&nbsp;    </span></b><br /><h4><i><span style="font-weight: normal;">*Job incorrectly states that it was God who took from him. But he didn't have John 10 to read and so the bible records that Job did not sin or charge God with wrong because he didn't blame God for abusing him without cause... <b>YET</b>...* </span></i></h4><h4>Attack 2 His body but not his life...</h4><div style="text-align: center;"><b>Chapter Two</b></div><span class="versetext" id="job2-1" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">1</span>    Again there was a day when the sons of God came to present  themselves before the Lord, and Satan came also among them to present  himself before the Lord.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job2-2" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">2</span>    And the Lord said to Satan, "From where do you come?" So Satan  answered the Lord and said, "From going to and fro on the earth, and  from walking back and forth on it."    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job2-3" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">3</span>    Then the Lord said to Satan, "Have you considered My servant Job,  that there is none like him on the earth, <b>a blameless and upright man, </b>one who fears God and shuns evil? And still he holds fast to his  integrity, although you incited Me against him, to destroy him without  cause."    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job2-4" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">4</span>    So Satan answered the Lord and said, "Skin for skin! Yes, all that a man has he will give for his life.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job2-5" style="display: inline;"><u><b><span class="versenum">5</span>    But stretch out Your hand now, and touch his bone and his flesh, and he will surely <span style="font-size: large;">curse </span>You to Your face!"</b></u>   </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job2-6" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">6</span>    And the Lord said to Satan, "Behold, he is in your hand, but spare his life."    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job2-7" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">7</span>    So Satan went out from the presence of the Lord, and struck Job with  painful boils from the sole of his foot to the crown of his head.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job2-8" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">8</span>    And he took for himself a potsherd with which to scrape himself while he sat in the midst of the ashes.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job2-9" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">9</span>    <u><b>Then his wife said to him, "Do you still hold fast to your integrity? <span style="font-size: large;">Curse </span>God and die!"    </b></u></span><u><b>          </b></u><span class="versetext" id="job2-10" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">10</span>    But he said to her, "You speak as one of the foolish women speaks.  Shall we indeed accept good from God, and shall we not accept  adversity?" In all this Job did not sin with his lips.</span><br /><br /><i>*Here we see that Satan is still after that curse and that cursing God has been on his Wife's mind too. Probably as a result of him talking about it with her before he did his fear based sacrifices.*</i><br /><br /><i>* We also notice that although Job has opened the door to the enemy through fear God, in His mercy, places limits on what Satan can or cannot do even then.* </i><br /><h4>The Three Friends Come to Visit</h4><br /><span class="versetext" id="job2-10" style="display: inline;">   </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job2-11" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">11</span>    Now when Job's three friends heard of all this adversity that had  come upon him, each one came from his own place--Eliphaz the Temanite,  Bildad the Shuhite, and Zophar the Naamathite. For they had made an  appointment together to come and mourn with him, and to comfort him.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job2-12" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">12</span>    And when they raised their eyes from afar, and did not recognize  him, they lifted their voices and wept; and each one tore his robe and  sprinkled dust on his head toward heaven.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job2-13" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">13</span>    So they sat down with him on the ground seven days and seven nights,  and no one spoke a word to him, for they saw that his grief was very  great.&nbsp;</span><br /><br /><span class="versetext" id="job2-13" style="display: inline;"><i>*Misery Loves Company! How often have I or other co-workers left a bad or crazy situation and just wanted to tell someone else who was a peer about it so we could both wallow in how bad that situation was... Here come Larry, Moe, and Curly to save the day and heap unfair abuse onto Job and pour salt into his wound. If Satan can't get you to curse God with circumstances, or family pressure, he'll try to use friends to heap condemnation on you until you wallow in how unfair it is and then curse God because of the unfairness...* </i></span><br /><h4>The Dialogues</h4><br /><i>*I will Copy and Paste all the words spoken by any character together. I'm looking for the themes of each persons speech and the content of his character.*</i><br /><div style="text-align: center;"></div><h4 style="text-align: center;"><b>JOB's Speeches </b></h4><span class="versetext" id="job3-1" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum"><i>Three-Job Opens his Complaint</i></span></span><br /><span class="versetext" id="job3-1" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">1</span>    After this Job opened his mouth and <b><span style="font-size: large;">cursed </span>the day of his birth</b>.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job3-2" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">2</span>    And Job spoke, and said:    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job3-3" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">3</span>    "May the day perish on which I was born, And the night in which it was said, 'A male child is conceived.'    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job3-4" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">4</span>    May that day be darkness; May God above not seek it, Nor the light shine upon it.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job3-5" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">5</span>    May darkness and the shadow of death claim it; May a cloud settle on it; May the blackness of the day terrify it.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job3-6" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">6</span>    As for that night, may darkness seize it; May it not rejoice <a href="http://draft.blogger.com/null" name="a"></a> among the days of the year, May it not come into the number of the months.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job3-7" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">7</span>    Oh, may that night be barren! May no joyful shout come into it!    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job3-8" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">8</span>    May those curse it who curse the day, Those who are ready to arouse Leviathan.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job3-9" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">9</span>    May the stars of its morning be dark; May it look for light, but have none, And not see the dawning of the day;    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job3-10" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">10</span>    Because it did not shut up the doors of my mother's womb, Nor hide sorrow from my eyes.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job3-11" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">11</span>    "Why did I not die at birth? Why did I not perish when I came from the womb?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job3-12" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">12</span>    Why did the knees receive me? Or why the breasts, that I should nurse?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job3-13" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">13</span>    For now I would have lain still and been quiet, I would have been asleep; Then I would have been at rest    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job3-14" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">14</span>    With kings and counselors of the earth, Who built ruins for themselves,    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job3-15" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">15</span>    Or with princes who had gold, Who filled their houses with silver;    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job3-16" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">16</span>    Or why was I not hidden like a stillborn child, Like infants who never saw light?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job3-17" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">17</span>    There the wicked cease from troubling, And there the weary are at rest.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job3-18" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">18</span>    There the prisoners rest together; <b>They do not hear the voice of the oppressor.</b>   </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job3-19" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">19</span>    The small and great are there, And the servant is free from his master.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job3-20" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">20</span>    "Why is light given to him who is in misery, And life to the bitter of soul,    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job3-21" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">21</span>    Who long for death, but it does not come, And search for it more than hidden treasures;    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job3-22" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">22</span>    Who rejoice exceedingly, And are glad when they can find the grave?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job3-23" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">23</span>    Why is light given to a man whose way is hidden, And whom God has hedged in?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job3-24" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">24</span>    For my sighing comes before I eat, <a href="http://draft.blogger.com/null" name="b"></a> And my groanings pour out like water. <u><b>   </b></u></span><u><b>          <span class="versetext" id="job3-25" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">25</span>    For the thing I greatly feared has come upon me, And what I dreaded has happened to me.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job3-26" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">26</span>    I am not at ease, nor am I quiet; I have no rest, for trouble comes."&nbsp; </span></b></u><br /><span class="versetext" id="job3-26" style="display: inline;"><i>*Fear always evokes the Curse. All that does not proceed from Faith is Sin. <a href="http://biblehub.com/romans/14-23.htm" target="_blank">Romans 14:32</a>. Therefore this act of engaging fear through constant sacrifices is sin. That sin opens the door to the enemy.* </i></span><br /><span class="versetext" id="job3-26" style="display: inline;"><i>Six Job Answers Eliphaz</i></span><br /><span class="versetext" id="job3-26" style="display: inline;"><span class="versetext" id="job6-1" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">1</span>    Then Job answered and said:    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job6-2" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">2</span>    "Oh, that my grief were fully weighed, And my calamity laid with it on the scales!    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job6-3" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">3</span>    For then it would be heavier than the sand of the sea-- Therefore my words have been rash.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job6-4" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">4</span>    <u><b>For the arrows of the Almighty are within me; My spirit drinks in their poison; The terrors of God are arrayed against me.</b></u>   </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job6-5" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">5</span>    Does the wild donkey bray when it has grass, Or does the ox low over its fodder?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job6-6" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">6</span>    Can flavorless food be eaten without salt? Or is there any taste in the white of an egg?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job6-7" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">7</span>    My soul refuses to touch them; They are as loathsome food to me.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job6-8" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">8</span>    "Oh, that I might have my request, That God would grant me the thing that I long for!    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job6-9" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">9</span>    That it would please God to crush me, That He would loose His hand and cut me off!    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job6-10" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">10</span>    Then I would still have comfort; Though in anguish, I would exult,  He will not spare; For I have not concealed the words of the Holy One.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job6-11" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">11</span>    "What strength do I have, that I should hope? And what is my end, that I should prolong my life?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job6-12" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">12</span>    Is my strength the strength of stones? Or is my flesh bronze?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job6-13" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">13</span>    Is my help not within me? And is success driven from me?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job6-14" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">14</span>    "To him who is afflicted, kindness should be shown by his friend, Even though he forsakes the fear of the Almighty.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job6-15" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">15</span>    My brothers have dealt deceitfully like a brook, Like the streams of the brooks that pass away,    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job6-16" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">16</span>    Which are dark because of the ice, And into which the snow vanishes.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job6-17" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">17</span>    When it is warm, they cease to flow; When it is hot, they vanish from their place.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job6-18" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">18</span>    The paths of their way turn aside, They go nowhere and perish.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job6-19" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">19</span>    The caravans of Tema look, The travelers of Sheba hope for them.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job6-20" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">20</span>    They are disappointed because they were confident; They come there and are confused.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job6-21" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">21</span>    For now you are nothing, You see terror and are afraid.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job6-22" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">22</span>    Did I ever say, 'Bring something to me'? Or, 'Offer a bribe for me from your wealth'?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job6-23" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">23</span>    Or, 'Deliver me from the enemy's hand'? Or, 'Redeem me from the hand of oppressors'?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job6-24" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">24</span>    "Teach me, and I will hold my tongue; Cause me to understand wherein I have erred.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job6-25" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">25</span>    How forceful are right words! But what does your arguing prove?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job6-26" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">26</span>    Do you intend to rebuke my words, And the speeches of a desperate one, which are as wind?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job6-27" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">27</span>    Yes, you overwhelm the fatherless, And you undermine your friend.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job6-28" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">28</span>    Now therefore, be pleased to look at me; For I would never lie to your face.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job6-29" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">29</span>    Yield now, let there be no injustice! Yes, concede, my righteousness still stands!    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job6-30" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">30</span>    Is there injustice on my tongue? Cannot my taste discern the unsavory?&nbsp; </span></span><br /><i><span class="versetext" id="job3-26" style="display: inline;"><span class="versetext" id="job6-30" style="display: inline;">Job didn't have all the revelation we have today, but we are still guilty of this misappropriation of blame. We blame God for Satan's work. Satan has poisonous arrows, not God. (<a href="http://www.biblestudytools.com/nkjv/ephesians/6-16.html" target="_blank">Ephesians 6:16</a>) Attacks are never from God, nor are they His will for us. But he may allow them to come earlier rather than later because the longer you wait to correct a child who is out of order the worse it will be for the child and harder it is to bring the change that is needed. Obviously the change was so profound in Job that he was double blessed in the end.*</span></span></i><br /><i><span class="versetext" id="job3-26" style="display: inline;"><span class="versetext" id="job6-30" style="display: inline;">Seven </span></span></i><span class="versetext" id="job3-26" style="display: inline;"><i>Job Answers Eliphaz Cont...</i></span><br /><span class="versetext" id="job3-26" style="display: inline;"><span class="versetext" id="job6-30" style="display: inline;"><span class="versetext" id="job7-1" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">1</span>    "Is there not a time of hard service for man on earth? Are not his days also like the days of a hired man?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job7-2" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">2</span>    Like a servant who earnestly desires the shade, And like a hired man who eagerly looks for his wages,    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job7-3" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">3</span>    <b>So I have been allotted months of futility</b>, And wearisome nights have been appointed to me.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job7-4" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">4</span>    When I lie down, I say, 'When shall I arise, And the night be ended?' For I have had my fill of tossing till dawn.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job7-5" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">5</span>    My flesh is caked with worms and dust, My skin is cracked and breaks out afresh.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job7-6" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">6</span>    "My days are swifter than a weaver's shuttle, And are spent without hope.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job7-7" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">7</span>    Oh, remember that my life is a breath! My eye will never again see good.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job7-8" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">8</span>    The eye of him who sees me will see me no more; While your eyes are upon me, I shall no longer be.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job7-9" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">9</span>    As the cloud disappears and vanishes away, So he who goes down to the grave does not come up.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job7-10" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">10</span>    He shall never return to his house, Nor shall his place know him anymore.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job7-11" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">11</span>    "Therefore I will not restrain my mouth; I will speak in the anguish  of my spirit; I will complain in the bitterness of my soul.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job7-12" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">12</span>    Am I a sea, or a sea serpent, That You set a guard over me?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job7-13" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">13</span>    When I say, 'My bed will comfort me, My couch will ease my complaint,'    </span>          <u><b><span class="versetext" id="job7-14" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">14</span>    Then You scare me with dreams And terrify me with visions,    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job7-15" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">15</span>    So that my soul chooses strangling And death rather than my body. <a href="http://draft.blogger.com/null" name="a"></a>   </span>          </b></u><span class="versetext" id="job7-16" style="display: inline;"><u><b><span class="versenum">16</span>    I loathe my life; I would not live forever.</b></u> Let me alone, For my days are but a breath.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job7-17" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">17</span>    "What is man, that You should exalt him, That You should set Your heart on him,    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job7-18" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">18</span>    That You should visit him every morning, And test him every moment?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job7-19" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">19</span>    How long? Will You not look away from me, And let me alone till I swallow my saliva?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job7-20" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">20</span>    Have I sinned? What have I done to You, O watcher of men? Why have  You set me as Your target, So that I am a burden to myself? <a href="http://draft.blogger.com/null" name="b"></a>   </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job7-21" style="display: inline;"><u><b><span class="versenum">21</span>    Why then do You not pardon my transgression, And take away my  iniquity? </b></u>For now I will lie down in the dust, And You will seek me  diligently, But I will no longer be."&nbsp;    </span>&nbsp;</span></span><br /><br /><i><span class="versetext" id="job3-26" style="display: inline;"><span class="versetext" id="job6-30" style="display: inline;">* Job's plight wasn't years it was months. I believe that it may have been about three to nine months.* Satan is plaguing Job's dreams with nightmares of fear and Job is attributing them to God.* *Job asks for a pardon for his Iniquity, so the Spirit of God is prompting him in the right direction, which we see in the speech of Elihu*</span></span></i><br /><br /><i><span class="versetext" id="job3-26" style="display: inline;"><span class="versetext" id="job6-30" style="display: inline;">Nine </span></span></i><span class="versetext" id="job3-26" style="display: inline;"><i>Job Answers Bildad</i></span><br /><span class="versetext" id="job9-1" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">1</span>    Then Job answered and said:    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job9-2" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">2</span>    "Truly I know it is so, <u><b>But how can a man be righteous before God?</b></u>   </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job9-3" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">3</span>    If one wished to contend with Him, He could not answer Him one time out of a thousand.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job9-4" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">4</span>    God is wise in heart and mighty in strength. Who has hardened himself against Him and prospered?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job9-5" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">5</span>    He removes the mountains, and they do not know When He overturns them in His anger;    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job9-6" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">6</span>    He shakes the earth out of its place, And its pillars tremble;    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job9-7" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">7</span>    He commands the sun, and it does not rise; He seals off the stars;    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job9-8" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">8</span>    He alone spreads out the heavens, And treads on the waves of the sea; <u>   </u></span><u>          </u><span class="versetext" id="job9-9" style="display: inline;"><u><b><span class="versenum">9</span>    He made the Bear, Orion, and the Pleiades, And the chambers of the south</b>;</u>   </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job9-10" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">10</span>    He does great things past finding out, Yes, wonders without number.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job9-11" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">11</span>    If He goes by me, I do not see Him; If He moves past, I do not perceive Him;    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job9-12" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">12</span>    If He takes away, who can hinder Him? Who can say to Him, 'What are You doing?'    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job9-13" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">13</span>    God will not withdraw His anger, The allies of the proud <a href="http://draft.blogger.com/null" name="a"></a> lie prostrate beneath Him.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job9-14" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">14</span>    "How then can I answer Him, And choose my words to reason with Him?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job9-15" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">15</span>    For though I were righteous, I could not answer Him; I would beg mercy of my Judge.    </span>          <u><b><span class="versetext" id="job9-16" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">16</span>    If I called and He answered me, I would not believe that He was listening to my voice.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job9-17" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">17</span>    For He crushes me with a tempest, And multiplies my wounds without cause.    </span></b></u>          <span class="versetext" id="job9-18" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">18</span>    He will not allow me to catch my breath, But fills me with bitterness.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job9-19" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">19</span>    If it is a matter of strength, indeed He is strong; And if of justice, who will appoint my day in court?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job9-20" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">20</span>    Though I were righteous, my own mouth would condemn me; Though I were blameless, it would prove me perverse.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job9-21" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">21</span>    "I am blameless, yet I do not know myself; I despise my life.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job9-22" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">22</span>    It is all one thing; <u><b>Therefore I say, 'He destroys the blameless and the wicked.'    </b></u></span><u><b>          </b></u><span class="versetext" id="job9-23" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">23</span>    If the scourge slays suddenly, He laughs at the plight of the innocent.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job9-24" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">24</span>    The earth is given into the hand of the wicked. He covers the faces of its judges. <u><b>If it is not He, who else could it be?</b></u>   </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job9-25" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">25</span>    "Now my days are swifter than a runner; They flee away, they see no good.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job9-26" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">26</span>    They pass by like swift ships, Like an eagle swooping on its prey.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job9-27" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">27</span>    If I say, 'I will forget my complaint, I will put off my sad face and wear a smile,'    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job9-28" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">28</span>    I am afraid of all my sufferings; I know that You will not hold me innocent.    </span>          <b><span class="versetext" id="job9-29" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">29</span>    If I am condemned, Why then do I labor in vain?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job9-30" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">30</span>    If I wash myself with snow water, And cleanse my hands with soap,    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job9-31" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">31</span>    Yet You will plunge me into the pit, And my own clothes will abhor me.    </span>          <u><span class="versetext" id="job9-32" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">32</span>    "For He is not a man, as I am, That I may answer Him, And that we should go to court together.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job9-33" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">33</span>    Nor is there any mediator between us, Who may lay his hand on us both.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job9-34" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">34</span>    Let Him take His rod away from me, And do not let dread of Him terrify me.    </span>          </u></b><span class="versetext" id="job9-35" style="display: inline;"><u><b><span class="versenum">35</span>    Then I would speak and not fear Him, But it is not so with me</b></u>. </span><br /><br /><i>* How can a man be righteous before God? Job has been defending his own righteousness and yet he knows that NO MAN can be righteous before God. With NT perspective we can see that ALL have fallen short of the glory of God. (<a href="http://www.biblestudytools.com/kjv/romans/3-23.html" target="_blank">Romans 3:23</a>)*</i><br /><i>* Job knew all about stars and star based navigation already.*</i><br /><i>*Job begins to accuse God of causing the wound and doing it without just cause. God destroys the righteous? I think not.*</i><br /><i>*Verse 24 is a KEY. If God isn't the one doing this, who could it be? Modern insurance companies call disasters "acts of God" when in fact they are "acts of Satan!". Even Modern Insurance Companies struggle with the same ignorance Job is dealing with. Every GOOD and PERFECT gift comes from God, (<a href="http://www.biblestudytools.com/kjv/james/1-17.html" target="_blank">Jame 1:17</a>) and we know that Jesus comes so that we may have ABUNDANT LIFE, but Satan comes to steal, Kill, and Destroy! (<a href="http://www.biblestudytools.com/kjv/john/10-10.html" target="_blank">John 10:10</a>). Good God, Bad Devil. If you can remember that you can find whole new and better understanding of this book and the life of Job.*</i><br /><i>*V 32-33 begins the process of understanding the reason this book is written and the struggle of Job. In fact the Fear Job engaged in that opened the door to Satan was itself the fruit, not the root, of the real problem. We discover that during all of Job's speeches he struggles with how a good person like him could have bad things happen to him. More-over he accuses God of being unfair because after all who could be as righteous as Job? In fact, if you study out the life of Job you find that there probably never has been anyone who was as outwardly righteous as Job, maybe not even as inwardly righteous as Job, but being the best there is, doesn't mean we hold to God's standard which is perfection. Only Jesus was truly righteous by God's standard. So Job calls for a man, a mediator, who understands the plight of man. That inward calling for a mediator is answered by Elihu in his speech, and ultimately fulfilled in the life of Jesus. You can come to Jesus without Fear, because he knows, intimately and first hand, how hard is to live life on this earth for God.*</i><br /><br /><i>Ten...</i><br /><span class="versetext" id="job10-1" style="display: inline;"><u><b><span class="versenum">1</span>    "My soul loathes my life; I will give free course to my complaint, I will speak in the bitterness of my soul.</b></u>   </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job10-2" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">2</span>    I will say to God, 'Do not condemn me; Show me why You contend with me.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job10-3" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">3</span>    Does it seem good to You that You should oppress, That You should  despise the work of Your hands, And smile on the counsel of the wicked?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job10-4" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">4</span>    Do You have eyes of flesh? Or do You see as man sees?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job10-5" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">5</span>    Are Your days like the days of a mortal man? Are Your years like the days of a mighty man,    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job10-6" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">6</span>    That You should seek for my iniquity And search out my sin,    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job10-7" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">7</span>    Although You know that I am not wicked, And there is no one who can deliver from Your hand?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job10-8" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">8</span>    'Your hands have made me and fashioned me, An intricate unity; Yet You would destroy me.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job10-9" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">9</span>    Remember, I pray, that You have made me like clay. And will You turn me into dust again?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job10-10" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">10</span>    Did you not pour me out like milk, And curdle me like cheese,    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job10-11" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">11</span>    Clothe me with skin and flesh, And knit me together with bones and sinews?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job10-12" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">12</span>    You have granted me life and favor, And Your care has preserved my spirit.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job10-13" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">13</span>    'And these things You have hidden in Your heart; I know that this was with You:    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job10-14" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">14</span>    If I sin, then You mark me, And will not acquit me of my iniquity.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job10-15" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">15</span>    If I am wicked, woe to me; Even if I am righteous, I cannot lift up my head. I am full of disgrace; See my misery!    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job10-16" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">16</span>    If my head is exalted, You hunt me like a fierce lion, And again You show Yourself awesome against me.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job10-17" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">17</span>    You renew Your witnesses against me, And increase Your indignation toward me; Changes and war are ever with me.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job10-18" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">18</span>    'Why then have You brought me out of the womb? Oh, that I had perished and no eye had seen me!    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job10-19" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">19</span>    I would have been as though I had not been. I would have been carried from the womb to the grave.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job10-20" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">20</span>    Are not my days few? Cease! Leave me alone, that I may take a little comfort,    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job10-21" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">21</span>    Before I go to the place from which I shall not return, To the land of darkness and the shadow of death,    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job10-22" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">22</span>    A land as dark as darkness itself, As the shadow of death, without any order, Where even the light is like darkness.' "    </span><br /><br /><i>* Job now opens up his complaint. I'm not saying he doesn't have good reason, but this is really the adult version of throwing a fit. He acknowledges God made him and intimately formed him and yet wishes he'd never been born. I sense here a profound lack of gratitude for the YEARS of good God gave him and the calling God had on his life.*</i><br /><br /><i>Twelve Job Answers Zophar</i><br /><span class="versetext" id="job12-1" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">1</span>    Then Job answered and said:    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job12-2" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">2</span>    "No doubt you are the people, And wisdom will die with you!    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job12-3" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">3</span>    But I have understanding as well as you; I am not inferior to you. Indeed, who does not know such things as these?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job12-4" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">4</span>    "I am one mocked by his friends, Who called on God, and He answered him, The just and blameless who is ridiculed.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job12-5" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">5</span>    A lamp <a href="http://draft.blogger.com/null" name="a"></a> is despised in the thought of one who is at ease; It is made ready for those whose feet slip.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job12-6" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">6</span>    The tents of robbers prosper, And those who provoke God are secure-- In what God provides by His hand.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job12-7" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">7</span>    "But now ask the beasts, and they will teach you; And the birds of the air, and they will tell you;    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job12-8" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">8</span>    Or speak to the earth, and it will teach you; And the fish of the sea will explain to you.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job12-9" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">9</span>    Who among all these does not know That the hand of the Lord has done this,    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job12-10" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">10</span>    In whose hand is the life of every living thing, And the breath of all mankind?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job12-11" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">11</span>    Does not the ear test words And the mouth taste its food?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job12-12" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">12</span>    Wisdom is with aged men, And with length of days, understanding.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job12-13" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">13</span>    "With Him are wisdom and strength, He has counsel and understanding.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job12-14" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">14</span>    If He breaks a thing down, it cannot be rebuilt; If He imprisons a man, there can be no release.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job12-15" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">15</span>    If He withholds the waters, they dry up; If He sends them out, they overwhelm the earth.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job12-16" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">16</span>    With Him are strength and prudence. The deceived and the deceiver are His.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job12-17" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">17</span>    He leads counselors away plundered, And makes fools of the judges.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job12-18" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">18</span>    He loosens the bonds of kings, And binds their waist with a belt.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job12-19" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">19</span>    He leads princes <a href="http://draft.blogger.com/null" name="b"></a> away plundered, And overthrows the mighty.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job12-20" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">20</span>    He deprives the trusted ones of speech, And takes away the discernment of the elders.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job12-21" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">21</span>    He pours contempt on princes, And disarms the mighty.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job12-22" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">22</span>    He uncovers deep things out of darkness, And brings the shadow of death to light.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job12-23" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">23</span>    He makes nations great, and destroys them; He enlarges nations, and guides them.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job12-24" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">24</span>    He takes away the understanding <a href="http://draft.blogger.com/null" name="c"></a> of the chiefs of the people of the earth, And makes them wander in a pathless wilderness.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job12-25" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">25</span>    They grope in the dark without light, And He makes them stagger like a drunken man.    </span><br /><i>Thirteen reply to Zophar Cont...</i><br /><span class="versetext" id="job13-1" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">1</span>    "Behold, my eye has seen all this, My ear has heard and understood it.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job13-2" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">2</span>    What you know, I also know; I am not inferior to you.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job13-3" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">3</span>    But I would speak to the Almighty, And I desire to reason with God.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job13-4" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">4</span>    But you forgers of lies, You are all worthless physicians.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job13-5" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">5</span>    Oh, that you would be silent, And it would be your wisdom!    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job13-6" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">6</span>    Now hear my reasoning, And heed the pleadings of my lips.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job13-7" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">7</span>    Will you speak wickedly for God, And talk deceitfully for Him?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job13-8" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">8</span>    Will you show partiality for Him? Will you contend for God?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job13-9" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">9</span>    Will it be well when He searches you out? Or can you mock Him as one mocks a man?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job13-10" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">10</span>    He will surely rebuke you If you secretly show partiality.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job13-11" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">11</span>    Will not His excellence make you afraid, And the dread of Him fall upon you?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job13-12" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">12</span>    Your platitudes are proverbs of ashes, Your defenses are defenses of clay.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job13-13" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">13</span>    "Hold your peace with me, and let me speak, Then let come on me what may!    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job13-14" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">14</span>    Why do I take my flesh in my teeth, And put my life in my hands?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job13-15" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">15</span>    Though He slay me, yet will I trust Him. Even so, I will defend my own ways before Him.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job13-16" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">16</span>    He also shall be my salvation, For a hypocrite could not come before Him.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job13-17" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">17</span>    Listen carefully to my speech, And to my declaration with your ears.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job13-18" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">18</span>    See now, I have prepared my case, I know that I shall be vindicated.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job13-19" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">19</span>    Who is he who will contend with me? If now I hold my tongue, I perish.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job13-20" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">20</span>    "Only two things do not do to me, Then I will not hide myself from You:    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job13-21" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">21</span>    Withdraw Your hand far from me, And let not the dread of You make me afraid.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job13-22" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">22</span>    Then call, and I will answer; Or let me speak, then You respond to me.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job13-23" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">23</span>    How many are my iniquities and sins? Make me know my transgression and my sin.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job13-24" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">24</span>    Why do You hide Your face, And regard me as Your enemy?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job13-25" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">25</span>    Will You frighten a leaf driven to and fro? And will You pursue dry stubble?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job13-26" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">26</span>    For You write bitter things against me, And make me inherit the iniquities of my youth.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job13-27" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">27</span>    You put my feet in the stocks, And watch closely all my paths. You set a limit <a href="http://draft.blogger.com/null" name="a"></a> for the soles of my feet.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job13-28" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">28</span>    "Man <a href="http://draft.blogger.com/null" name="b"></a> decays like a rotten thing, Like a garment that is moth-eaten.&nbsp;</span><br /><span class="versetext" id="job13-28" style="display: inline;"><i>Fourteen reply to Zophar Cont... </i></span><br /><span class="versetext" id="job14-1" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">1</span>    "Man who is born of woman Is of few days and full of trouble.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job14-2" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">2</span>    He comes forth like a flower and fades away; He flees like a shadow and does not continue.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job14-3" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">3</span>    And do You open Your eyes on such a one, And bring me <a href="http://draft.blogger.com/null" name="a"></a> to judgment with Yourself?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job14-4" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">4</span>    Who can bring a clean thing out of an unclean? No one!    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job14-5" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">5</span>    Since his days are determined, The number of his months is with You; You have appointed his limits, so that he cannot pass.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job14-6" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">6</span>    Look away from him that he may rest, Till like a hired man he finishes his day.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job14-7" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">7</span>    "For there is hope for a tree, If it is cut down, that it will sprout again, And that its tender shoots will not cease.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job14-8" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">8</span>    Though its root may grow old in the earth, And its stump may die in the ground,    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job14-9" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">9</span>    Yet at the scent of water it will bud And bring forth branches like a plant.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job14-10" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">10</span>    But man dies and is laid away; Indeed he breathes his last And where is he?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job14-11" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">11</span>    As water disappears from the sea, And a river becomes parched and dries up,    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job14-12" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">12</span>    So man lies down and does not rise. Till the heavens are no more, They will not awake Nor be roused from their sleep.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job14-13" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">13</span>    "Oh, that You would hide me in the grave, That You would conceal me  until Your wrath is past, That You would appoint me a set time, and  remember me!    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job14-14" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">14</span>    If a man dies, shall he live again? All the days of my hard service I will wait, Till my change comes.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job14-15" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">15</span>    You shall call, and I will answer You; You shall desire the work of Your hands.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job14-16" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">16</span>    For now You number my steps, But do not watch over my sin.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job14-17" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">17</span>    My transgression is sealed up in a bag, And You cover <a href="http://draft.blogger.com/null" name="b"></a> my iniquity.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job14-18" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">18</span>    "But as a mountain falls and crumbles away, And as a rock is moved from its place;    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job14-19" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">19</span>    As water wears away stones, And as torrents wash away the soil of the earth; So You destroy the hope of man.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job14-20" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">20</span>    You prevail forever against him, and he passes on; You change his countenance and send him away.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job14-21" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">21</span>    His sons come to honor, and he does not know it; They are brought low, and he does not perceive it.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job14-22" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">22</span>    But his flesh will be in pain over it, And his soul will mourn over it."    </span><br /><i>Sixteen Job Replied to Eliphaz second speech...</i><br /><span class="versetext" id="job16-1" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">1</span>    Then Job answered and said:    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job16-2" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">2</span>    "I have heard many such things; Miserable comforters are you all!    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job16-3" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">3</span>    Shall words of wind have an end? Or what provokes you that you answer?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job16-4" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">4</span>    I also could speak as you do, If your soul were in my soul's place. I  could heap up words against you, And shake my head at you;    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job16-5" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">5</span>    But I would strengthen you with my mouth, And the comfort of my lips would relieve your grief.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job16-6" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">6</span>    "Though I speak, my grief is not relieved; And if I remain silent, how am I eased?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job16-7" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">7</span>    But now He has worn me out; You have made desolate all my company.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job16-8" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">8</span>    You have shriveled me up, And it is a witness against me; My leanness rises up against me And bears witness to my face.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job16-9" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">9</span>    He tears me in His wrath, and hates me; He gnashes at me with His teeth; My adversary sharpens His gaze on me.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job16-10" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">10</span>    They gape at me with their mouth, They strike me reproachfully on the cheek, They gather together against me.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job16-11" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">11</span>    God has delivered me to the ungodly, And turned me over to the hands of the wicked.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job16-12" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">12</span>    I was at ease, but He has shattered me; He also has taken me by my  neck, and shaken me to pieces; He has set me up for His target,    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job16-13" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">13</span>    His archers surround me. He pierces my heart <a href="http://draft.blogger.com/null" name="a"></a> and does not pity; He pours out my gall on the ground.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job16-14" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">14</span>    He breaks me with wound upon wound; He runs at me like a warrior. <a href="http://draft.blogger.com/null" name="b"></a>   </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job16-15" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">15</span>    "I have sewn sackcloth over my skin, And laid my head <a href="http://draft.blogger.com/null" name="c"></a> in the dust.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job16-16" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">16</span>    My face is flushed from weeping, And on my eyelids is the shadow of death;    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job16-17" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">17</span>    Although no violence is in my hands, And my prayer is pure.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job16-18" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">18</span>    "O earth, do not cover my blood, And let my cry have no resting place!    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job16-19" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">19</span>    Surely even now my witness is in heaven, And my evidence is on high.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job16-20" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">20</span>    My friends scorn me; My eyes pour out tears to God.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job16-21" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">21</span>    Oh, that one might plead for a man with God, As a man pleads for his neighbor!    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job16-22" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">22</span>    For when a few years are finished, I shall go the way of no return. </span><br /><i>Seventeen Eliphaz#2 Reply Cont...</i><br /><span class="versetext" id="job17-1" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">1</span>    "My spirit is broken, My days are extinguished, The grave is ready for me.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job17-2" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">2</span>    Are not mockers with me? And does not my eye dwell on their provocation?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job17-3" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">3</span>    "Now put down a pledge for me with Yourself. Who is he who will shake hands with me?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job17-4" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">4</span>    For You have hidden their heart from understanding; Therefore You will not exalt them.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job17-5" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">5</span>    He who speaks flattery to his friends, Even the eyes of his children will fail.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job17-6" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">6</span>    "But He has made me a byword of the people, And I have become one in whose face men spit.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job17-7" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">7</span>    My eye has also grown dim because of sorrow, And all my members are like shadows.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job17-8" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">8</span>    Upright men are astonished at this, And the innocent stirs himself up against the hypocrite.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job17-9" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">9</span>    Yet the righteous will hold to his way, And he who has clean hands will be stronger and stronger.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job17-10" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">10</span>    "But please, come back again, all of you, <a href="http://draft.blogger.com/null" name="a"></a> For I shall not find one wise man among you.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job17-11" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">11</span>    My days are past, My purposes are broken off, Even the thoughts of my heart.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job17-12" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">12</span>    They change the night into day; 'The light is near,' they say, in the face of darkness.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job17-13" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">13</span>    If I wait for the grave as my house, If I make my bed in the darkness,    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job17-14" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">14</span>    If I say to corruption, 'You are my father,' And to the worm, 'You are my mother and my sister,'    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job17-15" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">15</span>    Where then is my hope? As for my hope, who can see it?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job17-16" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">16</span>    Will they go down to the gates of Sheol? Shall we have rest together in the dust?"    </span><br /><i>Nineteen Reply to Bildad's second speech</i><br /><span class="versetext" id="job19-1" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">1</span>    Then Job answered and said:    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job19-2" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">2</span>    "How long will you torment my soul, And break me in pieces with words?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job19-3" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">3</span>    These ten times you have reproached me; You are not ashamed that you have wronged me. <a href="http://draft.blogger.com/null" name="a"></a>   </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job19-4" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">4</span>    And if indeed I have erred, My error remains with me.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job19-5" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">5</span>    If indeed you exalt yourselves against me, And plead my disgrace against me,    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job19-6" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">6</span>    Know then that God has wronged me, And has surrounded me with His net.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job19-7" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">7</span>    "If I cry out concerning wrong, I am not heard. If I cry aloud, there is no justice.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job19-8" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">8</span>    He has fenced up my way, so that I cannot pass; And He has set darkness in my paths.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job19-9" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">9</span>    He has stripped me of my glory, And taken the crown from my head.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job19-10" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">10</span>    He breaks me down on every side, And I am gone; My hope He has uprooted like a tree.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job19-11" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">11</span>    He has also kindled His wrath against me, And He counts me as one of His enemies.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job19-12" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">12</span>    His troops come together And build up their road against me; They encamp all around my tent.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job19-13" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">13</span>    "He has removed my brothers far from me, And my acquaintances are completely estranged from me.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job19-14" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">14</span>    My relatives have failed, And my close friends have forgotten me.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job19-15" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">15</span>    Those who dwell in my house, and my maidservants, Count me as a stranger; I am an alien in their sight.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job19-16" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">16</span>    I call my servant, but he gives no answer; I beg him with my mouth.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job19-17" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">17</span>    My breath is offensive to my wife, And I am repulsive to the children of my own body.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job19-18" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">18</span>    Even young children despise me;
