He holds it fast, but it does not endure.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job8-16" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">16</span>    He grows green in the sun, And his branches spread out in his garden.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job8-17" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">17</span>    His roots wrap around the rock heap, And look for a place in the stones.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job8-18" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">18</span>    If he is destroyed from his place, Then it will deny him, saying, 'I have not seen you.'    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job8-19" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">19</span>    "Behold, this is the joy of His way, And out of the earth others will grow.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job8-20" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">20</span>    Behold, God will not cast away the blameless, Nor will He uphold the evildoers.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job8-21" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">21</span>    He will yet fill your mouth with laughing, And your lips with rejoicing.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job8-22" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">22</span>    Those who hate you will be clothed with shame, And the dwelling place of the wicked will come to nothing." </span><br /><i>Eighteen Bildad Second Speech</i><br /><span class="versetext" id="job18-1" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">1</span>    Then Bildad the Shuhite answered and said:    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job18-2" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">2</span>    "How long till you put an end to words? Gain understanding, and afterward we will speak.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job18-3" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">3</span>    Why are we counted as beasts, And regarded as stupid in your sight?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job18-4" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">4</span>    You who tear yourself in anger, Shall the earth be forsaken for you? Or shall the rock be removed from its place?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job18-5" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">5</span>    "The light of the wicked indeed goes out, And the flame of his fire does not shine.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job18-6" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">6</span>    The light is dark in his tent, And his lamp beside him is put out.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job18-7" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">7</span>    The steps of his strength are shortened, And his own counsel casts him down.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job18-8" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">8</span>    For he is cast into a net by his own feet, And he walks into a snare.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job18-9" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">9</span>    The net takes him by the heel, And a snare lays hold of him.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job18-10" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">10</span>    A noose is hidden for him on the ground, And a trap for him in the road.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job18-11" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">11</span>    Terrors frighten him on every side, And drive him to his feet.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job18-12" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">12</span>    His strength is starved, And destruction is ready at his side.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job18-13" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">13</span>    It devours patches of his skin; The firstborn of death devours his limbs.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job18-14" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">14</span>    He is uprooted from the shelter of his tent, And they parade him before the king of terrors.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job18-15" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">15</span>    They dwell in his tent who are none of his; Brimstone is scattered on his dwelling.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job18-16" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">16</span>    His roots are dried out below, And his branch withers above.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job18-17" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">17</span>    The memory of him perishes from the earth, And he has no name among the renowned. <a href="http://draft.blogger.com/null" name="a"></a>   </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job18-18" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">18</span>    He is driven from light into darkness, And chased out of the world.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job18-19" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">19</span>    He has neither son nor posterity among his people, Nor any remaining in his dwellings.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job18-20" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">20</span>    Those in the west are astonished at his day, As those in the east are frightened.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job18-21" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">21</span>    Surely such are the dwellings of the wicked, And this is the place of him who does not know God."&nbsp;</span><br /><i><span class="versetext" id="job18-21" style="display: inline;">&nbsp;Bildad takes a third stab at it</span></i><br /><i>Twenty Five</i><br /><span class="versetext" id="job25-1" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">1</span>    Then Bildad the Shuhite answered and said:    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job25-2" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">2</span>    "Dominion and fear belong to Him; He makes peace in His high places.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job25-3" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">3</span>    Is there any number to His armies? Upon whom does His light not rise?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job25-4" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">4</span>    How then can man be righteous before God? Or how can he be pure who is born of a woman?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job25-5" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">5</span>    If even the moon does not shine, And the stars are not pure in His sight,    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job25-6" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">6</span>    How much less man, who is a maggot, And a son of man, who is a worm?"    </span><br /><span class="versetext" id="job18-21" style="display: inline;">&nbsp;</span><b>&nbsp;</b><br /><h4 style="text-align: center;"><b>Zophar's Speeches</b></h4><i>Eleven</i><br /><span class="versetext" id="job11-1" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">1</span>    Then Zophar the Naamathite answered and said:    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job11-2" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">2</span>    "Should not the multitude of words be answered? And should a man full of talk be vindicated?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job11-3" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">3</span>    Should your empty talk make men hold their peace? And when you mock, should no one rebuke you?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job11-4" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">4</span>    For you have said, 'My doctrine is pure, And I am clean in your eyes.'    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job11-5" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">5</span>    But oh, that God would speak, And open His lips against you,    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job11-6" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">6</span>    That He would show you the secrets of wisdom! For they would double  your prudence. Know therefore that God exacts from you Less than your  iniquity deserves.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job11-7" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">7</span>    "Can you search out the deep things of God? Can you find out the limits of the Almighty?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job11-8" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">8</span>    They are higher than heaven--what can you do? Deeper than Sheol--what can you know?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job11-9" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">9</span>    Their measure is longer than the earth And broader than the sea.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job11-10" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">10</span>    "If He passes by, imprisons, and gathers to judgment, Then who can hinder Him?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job11-11" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">11</span>    For He knows deceitful men; He sees wickedness also. Will He not then consider it?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job11-12" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">12</span>    For an empty-headed man will be wise, When a wild donkey's colt is born a man.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job11-13" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">13</span>    "If you would prepare your heart, And stretch out your hands toward Him;    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job11-14" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">14</span>    If iniquity were in your hand, and you put it far away, And would not let wickedness dwell in your tents;    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job11-15" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">15</span>    Then surely you could lift up your face without spot; Yes, you could be steadfast, and not fear;    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job11-16" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">16</span>    Because you would forget your misery, And remember it as waters that have passed away,    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job11-17" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">17</span>    And your life would be brighter than noonday. Though you were dark, you would be like the morning.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job11-18" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">18</span>    And you would be secure, because there is hope; Yes, you would dig around you, and take your rest in safety.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job11-19" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">19</span>    You would also lie down, and no one would make you afraid; Yes, many would court your favor.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job11-20" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">20</span>    But the eyes of the wicked will fail, And they shall not escape, And their hope--loss of life!"    </span><br /><i>Twenty Zophar makes second speech</i><br /><span class="versetext" id="job20-1" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">1</span>    Then Zophar the Naamathite answered and said:    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job20-2" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">2</span>    "Therefore my anxious thoughts make me answer, Because of the turmoil within me.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job20-3" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">3</span>    I have heard the rebuke that reproaches me, And the spirit of my understanding causes me to answer.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job20-4" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">4</span>    "Do you not know this of old, Since man was placed on earth,    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job20-5" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">5</span>    That the triumphing of the wicked is short, And the joy of the hypocrite is but for a moment?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job20-6" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">6</span>    Though his haughtiness mounts up to the heavens, And his head reaches to the clouds,    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job20-7" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">7</span>    Yet he will perish forever like his own refuse; Those who have seen him will say, 'Where is he?'    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job20-8" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">8</span>    He will fly away like a dream, and not be found; Yes, he will be chased away like a vision of the night.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job20-9" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">9</span>    The eye that saw him will see him no more, Nor will his place behold him anymore.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job20-10" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">10</span>    His children will seek the favor of the poor, And his hands will restore his wealth.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job20-11" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">11</span>    His bones are full of his youthful vigor, But it will lie down with him in the dust.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job20-12" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">12</span>    "Though evil is sweet in his mouth, And he hides it under his tongue,    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job20-13" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">13</span>    Though he spares it and does not forsake it, But still keeps it in his mouth,    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job20-14" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">14</span>    Yet his food in his stomach turns sour; It becomes cobra venom within him.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job20-15" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">15</span>    He swallows down riches And vomits them up again; God casts them out of his belly.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job20-16" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">16</span>    He will suck the poison of cobras; The viper's tongue will slay him.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job20-17" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">17</span>    He will not see the streams, The rivers flowing with honey and cream.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job20-18" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">18</span>    He will restore that for which he labored, And will not swallow it  down; From the proceeds of business He will get no enjoyment.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job20-19" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">19</span>    For he has oppressed and forsaken the poor, He has violently seized a house which he did not build.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job20-20" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">20</span>    "Because he knows no quietness in his heart, <a href="http://draft.blogger.com/null" name="a"></a> He will not save anything he desires.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job20-21" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">21</span>    Nothing is left for him to eat; Therefore his well-being will not last.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job20-22" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">22</span>    In his self-sufficiency he will be in distress; Every hand of misery will come against him.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job20-23" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">23</span>    When he is about to fill his stomach, God will cast on him the fury  of His wrath, And will rain it on him while he is eating.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job20-24" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">24</span>    He will flee from the iron weapon; A bronze bow will pierce him through.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job20-25" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">25</span>    It is drawn, and comes out of the body; Yes, the glittering point comes out of his gall. Terrors come upon him;    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job20-26" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">26</span>    Total darkness is reserved for his treasures. An unfanned fire will  consume him; It shall go ill with him who is left in his tent.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job20-27" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">27</span>    The heavens will reveal his iniquity, And the earth will rise up against him.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job20-28" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">28</span>    The increase of his house will depart, And his goods will flow away in the day of His wrath.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job20-29" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">29</span>    This is the portion from God for a wicked man, The heritage appointed to him by God."    </span><br /><br /><h4>The Intercessor</h4><h4 style="text-align: center;">Elihu The Intercessor Speaks</h4><i>Thirty Two</i><br /><span class="versetext" id="job32-1" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">1</span>    So these three men ceased answering Job, because he was righteous in his own eyes.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job32-2" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">2</span>    Then the wrath of Elihu, the son of Barachel the Buzite, of the  family of Ram, was aroused against Job; his wrath was aroused because he  justified himself rather than God.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job32-3" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">3</span>    Also against his three friends his wrath was aroused, because they had found no answer, and yet had condemned Job.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job32-4" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">4</span>    Now because they were years older than he, Elihu had waited to speak to Job. <a href="http://draft.blogger.com/null" name="a"></a>   </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job32-5" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">5</span>    When Elihu saw that there was no answer in the mouth of these three men, his wrath was aroused.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job32-6" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">6</span>    So Elihu, the son of Barachel the Buzite, answered and said:  "I am  young in years, and you are very old; Therefore I was afraid, And dared  not declare my opinion to you.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job32-7" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">7</span>    I said, 'Age <a href="http://draft.blogger.com/null" name="b"></a> should speak, And multitude of years should teach wisdom.'    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job32-8" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">8</span>    But there is a spirit in man, And the breath of the Almighty gives him understanding.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job32-9" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">9</span>    Great men <a href="http://draft.blogger.com/null" name="c"></a> are not always wise, Nor do the aged always understand justice.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job32-10" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">10</span>    "Therefore I say, 'Listen to me, I also will declare my opinion.'    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job32-11" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">11</span>    Indeed I waited for your words, I listened to your reasonings, while you searched out what to say.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job32-12" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">12</span>    I paid close attention to you; And surely not one of you convinced Job, Or answered his words--    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job32-13" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">13</span>    Lest you say, 'We have found wisdom'; God will vanquish him, not man.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job32-14" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">14</span>    Now he has not directed his words against me; So I will not answer him with your words.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job32-15" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">15</span>    "They are dismayed and answer no more; Words escape them.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job32-16" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">16</span>    And I have waited, because they did not speak, Because they stood still and answered no more.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job32-17" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">17</span>    I also will answer my part, I too will declare my opinion.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job32-18" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">18</span>    For I am full of words; The spirit within me compels me.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job32-19" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">19</span>    Indeed my belly is like wine that has no vent; It is ready to burst like new wineskins.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job32-20" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">20</span>    I will speak, that I may find relief; I must open my lips and answer.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job32-21" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">21</span>    Let me not, I pray, show partiality to anyone; Nor let me flatter any man.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job32-22" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">22</span>    For I do not know how to flatter, Else my Maker would soon take me away.    </span><br /><i>Thirty Three</i><br /><span class="versetext" id="job33-1" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">1</span>    "But please, Job, hear my speech, And listen to all my words.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job33-2" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">2</span>    Now, I open my mouth; My tongue speaks in my mouth.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job33-3" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">3</span>    My words come from my upright heart; My lips utter pure knowledge.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job33-4" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">4</span>    The Spirit of God has made me, And the breath of the Almighty gives me life.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job33-5" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">5</span>    If you can answer me, Set your words in order before me; Take your stand.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job33-6" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">6</span>    Truly I am as your spokesman <a href="http://draft.blogger.com/null" name="a"></a> before God; I also have been formed out of clay.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job33-7" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">7</span>    Surely no fear of me will terrify you, Nor will my hand be heavy on you.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job33-8" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">8</span>    "Surely you have spoken in my hearing, And I have heard the sound of your words, saying,    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job33-9" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">9</span>    'I am pure, without transgression; I am innocent, and there is no iniquity in me.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job33-10" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">10</span>    Yet He finds occasions against me, He counts me as His enemy;    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job33-11" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">11</span>    He puts my feet in the stocks, He watches all my paths.'    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job33-12" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">12</span>    "Look, in this you are not righteous. I will answer you, For God is greater than man.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job33-13" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">13</span>    Why do you contend with Him? For He does not give an accounting of any of His words.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job33-14" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">14</span>    For God may speak in one way, or in another, Yet man does not perceive it.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job33-15" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">15</span>    In a dream, in a vision of the night, When deep sleep falls upon men, While slumbering on their beds,    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job33-16" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">16</span>    Then He opens the ears of men, And seals their instruction.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job33-17" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">17</span>    In order to turn man from his deed, And conceal pride from man,    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job33-18" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">18</span>    He keeps back his soul from the Pit, And his life from perishing by the sword.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job33-19" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">19</span>    "Man is also chastened with pain on his bed, And with strong pain in many of his bones,    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job33-20" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">20</span>    So that his life abhors bread, And his soul succulent food.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job33-21" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">21</span>    His flesh wastes away from sight, And his bones stick out which once were not seen.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job33-22" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">22</span>    Yes, his soul draws near the Pit, And his life to the executioners.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job33-23" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">23</span>    "If there is a messenger for him, A mediator, one among a thousand, To show man His uprightness,    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job33-24" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">24</span>    Then He is gracious to him, and says, 'Deliver him from going down to the Pit; I have found a ransom';    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job33-25" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">25</span>    His flesh shall be young like a child's, He shall return to the days of his youth.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job33-26" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">26</span>    He shall pray to God, and He will delight in him, He shall see His face with joy, For He restores to man His righteousness.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job33-27" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">27</span>    Then he looks at men and says, 'I have sinned, and perverted what was right, And it did not profit me.'    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job33-28" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">28</span>    He will redeem his <a href="http://draft.blogger.com/null" name="b"></a> soul from going down to the Pit, And his <a href="http://draft.blogger.com/null" name="c"></a> life shall see the light.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job33-29" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">29</span>    "Behold, God works all these things, Twice, in fact, three times with a man,    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job33-30" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">30</span>    To bring back his soul from the Pit, That he may be enlightened with the light of life.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job33-31" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">31</span>    "Give ear, Job, listen to me; Hold your peace, and I will speak.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job33-32" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">32</span>    If you have anything to say, answer me; Speak, for I desire to justify you.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job33-33" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">33</span>    If not, listen to me; Hold your peace, and I will teach you wisdom."    </span><br /><i>Thirty Four</i><br /><span class="versetext" id="job34-1" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">1</span>    Elihu further answered and said:    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job34-2" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">2</span>    "Hear my words, you wise men; Give ear to me, you who have knowledge.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job34-3" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">3</span>    For the ear tests words As the palate tastes food.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job34-4" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">4</span>    Let us choose justice for ourselves; Let us know among ourselves what is good.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job34-5" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">5</span>    "For Job has said, 'I am righteous, But God has taken away my justice;    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job34-6" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">6</span>    Should I lie concerning my right? My wound is incurable, though I am without transgression.'    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job34-7" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">7</span>    What man is like Job, Who drinks scorn like water,    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job34-8" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">8</span>    Who goes in company with the workers of iniquity, And walks with wicked men?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job34-9" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">9</span>    For he has said, 'It profits a man nothing That he should delight in God.'    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job34-10" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">10</span>    "Therefore listen to me, you men of understanding: Far be it from  God to do wickedness, And from the Almighty to commit iniquity.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job34-11" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">11</span>    For He repays man according to his work, And makes man to find a reward according to his way.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job34-12" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">12</span>    Surely God will never do wickedly, Nor will the Almighty pervert justice.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job34-13" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">13</span>    Who gave Him charge over the earth? Or who appointed Him over the whole world?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job34-14" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">14</span>    If He should set His heart on it, If He should gather to Himself His Spirit and His breath,    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job34-15" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">15</span>    All flesh would perish together, And man would return to dust.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job34-16" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">16</span>    "If you have understanding, hear this; Listen to the sound of my words:    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job34-17" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">17</span>    Should one who hates justice govern? Will you condemn Him who is most just?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job34-18" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">18</span>    Is it fitting to say to a king, 'You are worthless,' And to nobles, 'You are wicked'?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job34-19" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">19</span>    Yet He is not partial to princes, Nor does He regard the rich more than the poor; For they are all the work of His hands.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job34-20" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">20</span>    In a moment they die, in the middle of the night; The people are  shaken and pass away; The mighty are taken away without a hand.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job34-21" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">21</span>    "For His eyes are on the ways of man, And He sees all his steps.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job34-22" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">22</span>    There is no darkness nor shadow of death Where the workers of iniquity may hide themselves.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job34-23" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">23</span>    For He need not further consider a man, That he should go before God in judgment.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job34-24" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">24</span>    He breaks in pieces mighty men without inquiry, And sets others in their place.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job34-25" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">25</span>    Therefore he knows their works; He overthrows them in the night, And they are crushed.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job34-26" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">26</span>    He strikes them as wicked men In the open sight of others,    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job34-27" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">27</span>    Because they turned back from Him, And would not consider any of His ways,    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job34-28" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">28</span>    So that they caused the cry of the poor to come to Him; For He hears the cry of the afflicted.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job34-29" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">29</span>    When He gives quietness, who then can make trouble? And when He  hides His face, who then can see Him, Whether it is against a nation or a  man alone?--    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job34-30" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">30</span>    That the hypocrite should not reign, Lest the people be ensnared.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job34-31" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">31</span>    "For has anyone said to God, ' I have borne chastening; I will offend no more;    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job34-32" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">32</span>    Teach me what I do not see; If I have done iniquity, I will do no more'?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job34-33" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">33</span>    Should He repay it according to your terms, Just because you disavow  it? You must choose, and not I; Therefore speak what you know.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job34-34" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">34</span>    "Men of understanding say to me, Wise men who listen to me:    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job34-35" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">35</span>    'Job speaks without knowledge, His words are without wisdom.'    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job34-36" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">36</span>    Oh, that Job were tried to the utmost, Because his answers are like those of wicked men!    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job34-37" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">37</span>    For he adds rebellion to his sin; He claps his hands among us, And multiplies his words against God."&nbsp;</span><br /><i><span class="versetext" id="job34-37" style="display: inline;">Thirty Five</span></i><br /><span class="versetext" id="job34-37" style="display: inline;"><span class="versetext" id="job35-1" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">1</span>    Moreover Elihu answered and said:    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job35-2" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">2</span>    "Do you think this is right? Do you say, 'My righteousness is more than God's'?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job35-3" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">3</span>    For you say, 'What advantage will it be to You? What profit shall I have, more than if I had sinned?'    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job35-4" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">4</span>    "I will answer you, And your companions with you.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job35-5" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">5</span>    Look to the heavens and see; And behold the clouds-- They are higher than you.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job35-6" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">6</span>    If you sin, what do you accomplish against Him? Or, if your transgressions are multiplied, what do you do to Him?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job35-7" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">7</span>    If you are righteous, what do you give Him? Or what does He receive from your hand?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job35-8" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">8</span>    Your wickedness affects a man such as you, And your righteousness a son of man.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job35-9" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">9</span>    "Because of the multitude of oppressions they cry out; They cry out for help because of the arm of the mighty.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job35-10" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">10</span>    But no one says, 'Where is God my Maker, Who gives songs in the night,    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job35-11" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">11</span>    Who teaches us more than the beasts of the earth, And makes us wiser than the birds of heaven?'    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job35-12" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">12</span>    There they cry out, but He does not answer, Because of the pride of evil men.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job35-13" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">13</span>    Surely God will not listen to empty talk, Nor will the Almighty regard it.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job35-14" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">14</span>    Although you say you do not see Him, Yet justice is before Him, and you must wait for Him.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job35-15" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">15</span>    And now, because He has not punished in His anger, Nor taken much notice of folly,    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job35-16" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">16</span>    Therefore Job opens his mouth in vain; He multiplies words without knowledge."&nbsp;    </span></span><br /><i>Thirty Six</i><br /><span class="versetext" id="job36-1" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">1</span>    Elihu also proceeded and said:    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job36-2" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">2</span>    "Bear with me a little, and I will show you That there are yet words to speak on God's behalf.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job36-3" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">3</span>    I will fetch my knowledge from afar; I will ascribe righteousness to my Maker.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job36-4" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">4</span>    For truly my words are not false; One who is perfect in knowledge is with you.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job36-5" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">5</span>    "Behold, God is mighty, but despises no one; He is mighty in strength of understanding.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job36-6" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">6</span>    He does not preserve the life of the wicked, But gives justice to the oppressed.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job36-7" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">7</span>    He does not withdraw His eyes from the righteous; But they are on  the throne with kings, For He has seated them forever, And they are  exalted.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job36-8" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">8</span>    And if they are bound in fetters, Held in the cords of affliction,    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job36-9" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">9</span>    Then He tells them their work and their transgressions-- That they have acted defiantly.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job36-10" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">10</span>    He also opens their ear to instruction, And commands that they turn from iniquity.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job36-11" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">11</span>    If they obey and serve Him, They shall spend their days in prosperity, And their years in pleasures.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job36-12" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">12</span>    But if they do not obey, They shall perish by the sword, And they shall die without knowledge. <a href="http://draft.blogger.com/null" name="a"></a>   </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job36-13" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">13</span>    "But the hypocrites in heart store up wrath; They do not cry for help when He binds them.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job36-14" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">14</span>    They die in youth, And their life ends among the perverted persons. <a href="http://draft.blogger.com/null" name="b"></a>   </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job36-15" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">15</span>    He delivers the poor in their affliction, And opens their ears in oppression.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job36-16" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">16</span>    "Indeed He would have brought you out of dire distress, Into a broad  place where there is no restraint; And what is set on your table would  be full of richness.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job36-17" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">17</span>    But you are filled with the judgment due the wicked; Judgment and justice take hold of you.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job36-18" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">18</span>    Because there is wrath, beware lest He take you away with one blow; For a large ransom would not help you avoid it.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job36-19" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">19</span>    Will your riches, Or all the mighty forces, Keep you from distress?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job36-20" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">20</span>    Do not desire the night, When people are cut off in their place.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job36-21" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">21</span>    Take heed, do not turn to iniquity, For you have chosen this rather than affliction.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job36-22" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">22</span>    "Behold, God is exalted by His power; Who teaches like Him?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job36-23" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">23</span>    Who has assigned Him His way, Or who has said, 'You have done wrong'?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job36-24" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">24</span>    "Remember to magnify His work, Of which men have sung.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job36-25" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">25</span>    Everyone has seen it; Man looks on it from afar.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job36-26" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">26</span>    "Behold, God is great, and we do not know Him; Nor can the number of His years be discovered.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job36-27" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">27</span>    For He draws up drops of water, Which distill as rain from the mist,    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job36-28" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">28</span>    Which the clouds drop down And pour abundantly on man.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job36-29" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">29</span>    Indeed, can anyone understand the spreading of clouds, The thunder from His canopy?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job36-30" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">30</span>    Look, He scatters his light upon it, And covers the depths of the sea.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job36-31" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">31</span>    For by these He judges the peoples; He gives food in abundance.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job36-32" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">32</span>    He covers His hands with lightning, And commands it to strike.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job36-33" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">33</span>    His thunder declares it, The cattle also, concerning the rising storm.&nbsp;    </span><i><br /></i><br /><i>Thirty Seven</i><br /><span class="versetext" id="job37-1" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">1</span>    "At this also my heart trembles, And leaps from its place.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job37-2" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">2</span>    Hear attentively the thunder of His voice, And the rumbling that comes from His mouth.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job37-3" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">3</span>    He sends it forth under the whole heaven, His lightning to the ends of the earth.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job37-4" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">4</span>    After it a voice roars; He thunders with His majestic voice, And He does not restrain them when His voice is heard.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job37-5" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">5</span>    God thunders marvelously with His voice; He does great things which we cannot comprehend.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job37-6" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">6</span>    For He says to the snow, 'Fall on the earth'; Likewise to the gentle rain and the heavy rain of His strength.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job37-7" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">7</span>    He seals the hand of every man, That all men may know His work.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job37-8" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">8</span>    The beasts go into dens, And remain in their lairs.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job37-9" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">9</span>    From the chamber of the south comes the whirlwind, And cold from the scattering winds of the north.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job37-10" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">10</span>    By the breath of God ice is given, And the broad waters are frozen.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job37-11" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">11</span>    Also with moisture He saturates the thick clouds; He scatters His bright clouds.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job37-12" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">12</span>    And they swirl about, being turned by His guidance, That they may do  whatever He commands them On the face of the whole earth. <a href="http://draft.blogger.com/null" name="a"></a>   </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job37-13" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">13</span>    He causes it to come, Whether for correction, Or for His land, Or for mercy.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job37-14" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">14</span>    "Listen to this, O Job; Stand still and consider the wondrous works of God.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job37-15" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">15</span>    Do you know when God dispatches them, And causes the light of His cloud to shine?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job37-16" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">16</span>    Do you know how the clouds are balanced, Those wondrous works of Him who is perfect in knowledge?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job37-17" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">17</span>    Why are your garments hot, When He quiets the earth by the south wind?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job37-18" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">18</span>    With Him, have you spread out the skies, Strong as a cast metal mirror?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job37-19" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">19</span>    "Teach us what we should say to Him, For we can prepare nothing because of the darkness.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job37-20" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">20</span>    Should He be told that I wish to speak? If a man were to speak, surely he would be swallowed up.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job37-21" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">21</span>    Even now men cannot look at the light when it is bright in the skies, When the wind has passed and cleared them.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job37-22" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">22</span>    He comes from the north as golden splendor; With God is awesome majesty.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job37-23" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">23</span>    As for the Almighty, we cannot find Him; He is excellent in power, In judgment and abundant justice; He does not oppress.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job37-24" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">24</span>    Therefore men fear Him; He shows no partiality to any who are wise of heart."    </span><br /><br />GOD SPEAKS<br /><br /><i>Thirty Eight</i><br /><span class="versetext" id="job38-1" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">1</span>    Then the Lord answered Job out of the whirlwind, and said:    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job38-2" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">2</span>    "Who is this who darkens counsel By words without knowledge?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job38-3" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">3</span>    Now prepare yourself like a man; I will question you, and you shall answer Me.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job38-4" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">4</span>    "Where were you when I laid the foundations of the earth? Tell Me, if you have understanding.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job38-5" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">5</span>    Who determined its measurements? Surely you know! Or who stretched the line upon it?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job38-6" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">6</span>    To what were its foundations fastened? Or who laid its cornerstone,    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job38-7" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">7</span>    When the morning stars sang together, And all the sons of God shouted for joy?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job38-8" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">8</span>    "Or who shut in the sea with doors, When it burst forth and issued from the womb;    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job38-9" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">9</span>    When I made the clouds its garment, And thick darkness its swaddling band;    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job38-10" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">10</span>    When I fixed My limit for it, And set bars and doors;    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job38-11" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">11</span>    When I said, 'This far you may come, but no farther, And here your proud waves must stop!'    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job38-12" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">12</span>    "Have you commanded the morning since your days began, And caused the dawn to know its place,    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job38-13" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">13</span>    That it might take hold of the ends of the earth, And the wicked be shaken out of it?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job38-14" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">14</span>    It takes on form like clay under a seal, And stands out like a garment.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job38-15" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">15</span>    From the wicked their light is withheld, And the upraised arm is broken.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job38-16" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">16</span>    "Have you entered the springs of the sea? Or have you walked in search of the depths?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job38-17" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">17</span>    Have the gates of death been revealed to you? Or have you seen the doors of the shadow of death?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job38-18" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">18</span>    Have you comprehended the breadth of the earth? Tell Me, if you know all this.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job38-19" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">19</span>    "Where is the way to the dwelling of light? And darkness, where is its place,    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job38-20" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">20</span>    That you may take it to its territory, That you may know the paths to its home?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job38-21" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">21</span>    Do you know it, because you were born then, Or because the number of your days is great?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job38-22" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">22</span>    "Have you entered the treasury of snow, Or have you seen the treasury of hail,    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job38-23" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">23</span>    Which I have reserved for the time of trouble, For the day of battle and war?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job38-24" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">24</span>    By what way is light diffused, Or the east wind scattered over the earth?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job38-25" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">25</span>    "Who has divided a channel for the overflowing water, Or a path for the thunderbolt,    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job38-26" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">26</span>    To cause it to rain on a land where there is no one, A wilderness in which there is no man;    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job38-27" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">27</span>    To satisfy the desolate waste, And cause to spring forth the growth of tender grass?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job38-28" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">28</span>    Has the rain a father? Or who has begotten the drops of dew?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job38-29" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">29</span>    From whose womb comes the ice? And the frost of heaven, who gives it birth?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job38-30" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">30</span>    The waters harden like stone, And the surface of the deep is frozen.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job38-31" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">31</span>    "Can you bind the cluster of the Pleiades, Or loose the belt of Orion?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job38-32" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">32</span>    Can you bring out Mazzaroth <a href="http://draft.blogger.com/null" name="a"></a> in its season? Or can you guide the Great Bear with its cubs?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job38-33" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">33</span>    Do you know the ordinances of the heavens? Can you set their dominion over the earth?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job38-34" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">34</span>    "Can you lift up your voice to the clouds, That an abundance of water may cover you?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job38-35" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">35</span>    Can you send out lightnings, that they may go, And say to you, 'Here we are!'?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job38-36" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">36</span>    Who has put wisdom in the mind? <a href="http://draft.blogger.com/null" name="b"></a> Or who has given understanding to the heart?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job38-37" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">37</span>    Who can number the clouds by wisdom? Or who can pour out the bottles of heaven,    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job38-38" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">38</span>    When the dust hardens in clumps, And the clods cling together?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job38-39" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">39</span>    "Can you hunt the prey for the lion, Or satisfy the appetite of the young lions,    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job38-40" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">40</span>    When they crouch in their dens, Or lurk in their lairs to lie in wait?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job38-41" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">41</span>    Who provides food for the raven, When its young ones cry to God, And wander about for lack of food?    </span><br /><i>Thirty Nine</i><br /><span class="versetext" id="job39-1" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">1</span>    "Do you know the time when the wild mountain goats bear young? Or can you mark when the deer gives birth?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job39-2" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">2</span>    Can you number the months that they fulfill? Or do you know the time when they bear young?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job39-3" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">3</span>    They bow down, They bring forth their young, They deliver their offspring. <a href="http://draft.blogger.com/null" name="a"></a>   </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job39-4" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">4</span>    Their young ones are healthy, They grow strong with grain; They depart and do not return to them.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job39-5" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">5</span>    "Who set the wild donkey free? Who loosed the bonds of the onager,    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job39-6" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">6</span>    Whose home I have made the wilderness, And the barren land his dwelling?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job39-7" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">7</span>    He scorns the tumult of the city; He does not heed the shouts of the driver.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job39-8" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">8</span>    The range of the mountains is his pasture, And he searches after every green thing.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job39-9" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">9</span>    "Will the wild ox be willing to serve you? Will he bed by your manger?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job39-10" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">10</span>    Can you bind the wild ox in the furrow with ropes? Or will he plow the valleys behind you?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job39-11" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">11</span>    Will you trust him because his strength is great? Or will you leave your labor to him?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job39-12" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">12</span>    Will you trust him to bring home your grain, And gather it to your threshing floor?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job39-13" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">13</span>    "The wings of the ostrich wave proudly, But are her wings and pinions like the kindly stork's?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job39-14" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">14</span>    For she leaves her eggs on the ground, And warms them in the dust;    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job39-15" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">15</span>    She forgets that a foot may crush them, Or that a wild beast may break them.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job39-16" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">16</span>    She treats her young harshly, as though they were not hers; Her labor is in vain, without concern,    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job39-17" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">17</span>    Because God deprived her of wisdom, And did not endow her with understanding.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job39-18" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">18</span>    When she lifts herself on high, She scorns the horse and its rider.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job39-19" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">19</span>    "Have you given the horse strength? Have you clothed his neck with thunder? <a href="http://draft.blogger.com/null" name="b"></a>   </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job39-20" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">20</span>    Can you frighten him like a locust? His majestic snorting strikes terror.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job39-21" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">21</span>    He paws in the valley, and rejoices in his strength; He gallops into the clash of arms.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job39-22" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">22</span>    He mocks at fear, and is not frightened; Nor does he turn back from the sword.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job39-23" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">23</span>    The quiver rattles against him, The glittering spear and javelin.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job39-24" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">24</span>    He devours the distance with fierceness and rage; Nor does he come to a halt because the trumpet has sounded.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job39-25" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">25</span>    At the blast of the trumpet he says, 'Aha!' He smells the battle from afar, The thunder of captains and shouting.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job39-26" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">26</span>    "Does the hawk fly by your wisdom, And spread its wings toward the south?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job39-27" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">27</span>    Does the eagle mount up at your command, And make its nest on high?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job39-28" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">28</span>    On the rocks it dwells and resides, On the crag of the rock and the stronghold.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job39-29" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">29</span>    From there it spies out the prey; Its eyes observe from afar.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job39-30" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">30</span>    Its young ones suck up blood; And where the slain are, there it is."    </span><br /><i>Forty</i><br /><span class="versetext" id="job40-1" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">1</span>    Moreover the Lord answered Job, and said:    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job40-2" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">2</span>    "Shall the one who contends with the Almighty correct Him? He who rebukes God, let him answer it."    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job40-3" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">3</span>    Then Job answered the Lord and said:    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job40-4" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">4</span>    "Behold, I am vile; What shall I answer You? I lay my hand over my mouth.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job40-5" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">5</span>    Once I have spoken, but I will not answer; Yes, twice, but I will proceed no further."    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job40-6" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">6</span>    Then the Lord answered Job out of the whirlwind, and said:    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job40-7" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">7</span>    "Now prepare yourself like a man; I will question you, and you shall answer Me:    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job40-8" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">8</span>    "Would you indeed annul My judgment? Would you condemn Me that you may be justified?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job40-9" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">9</span>    Have you an arm like God? Or can you thunder with a voice like His?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job40-10" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">10</span>    Then adorn yourself with majesty and splendor, And array yourself with glory and beauty.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job40-11" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">11</span>    Disperse the rage of your wrath; Look on everyone who is proud, and humble him.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job40-12" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">12</span>    Look on everyone who is proud, and bring him low; Tread down the wicked in their place.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job40-13" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">13</span>    Hide them in the dust together, Bind their faces in hidden darkness.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job40-14" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">14</span>    Then I will also confess to you That your own right hand can save you.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job40-15" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">15</span>    "Look now at the behemoth, <a href="http://draft.blogger.com/null" name="a"></a> which I made along with you; He eats grass like an ox.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job40-16" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">16</span>    See now, his strength is in his hips, And his power is in his stomach muscles.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job40-17" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">17</span>    He moves his tail like a cedar; The sinews of his thighs are tightly knit.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job40-18" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">18</span>    His bones are like beams of bronze, His ribs like bars of iron.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job40-19" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">19</span>    He is the first of the ways of God; Only He who made him can bring near His sword.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job40-20" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">20</span>    Surely the mountains yield food for him, And all the beasts of the field play there.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job40-21" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">21</span>    He lies under the lotus trees, In a covert of reeds and marsh.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job40-22" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">22</span>    The lotus trees cover him with their shade; The willows by the brook surround him.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job40-23" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">23</span>    Indeed the river may rage, Yet he is not disturbed; He is confident, though the Jordan gushes into his mouth,    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job40-24" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">24</span>    Though he takes it in his eyes, Or one pierces his nose with a snare.&nbsp;</span><br /><span class="versetext" id="job40-24" style="display: inline;"><i>Forty One</i> </span><br /><span class="versetext" id="job41-1" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">1</span>    "Can you draw out Leviathan <a href="http://draft.blogger.com/null" name="a"></a> with a hook, Or snare his tongue with a line which you lower?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job41-2" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">2</span>    Can you put a reed through his nose, Or pierce his jaw with a hook?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job41-3" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">3</span>    Will he make many supplications to you? Will he speak softly to you?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job41-4" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">4</span>    Will he make a covenant with you? Will you take him as a servant forever?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job41-5" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">5</span>    Will you play with him as with a bird, Or will you leash him for your maidens?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job41-6" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">6</span>    Will your companions make a banquet <a href="http://draft.blogger.com/null" name="b"></a> of him? Will they apportion him among the merchants?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job41-7" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">7</span>    Can you fill his skin with harpoons, Or his head with fishing spears?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job41-8" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">8</span>    Lay your hand on him; Remember the battle-- Never do it again!    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job41-9" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">9</span>    Indeed, any hope of overcoming him is false; Shall one not be overwhelmed at the sight of him?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job41-10" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">10</span>    No one is so fierce that he would dare stir him up. Who then is able to stand against Me?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job41-11" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">11</span>    Who has preceded Me, that I should pay him? Everything under heaven is Mine.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job41-12" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">12</span>    "I will not conceal <a href="http://draft.blogger.com/null" name="c"></a> his limbs, His mighty power, or his graceful proportions.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job41-13" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">13</span>    Who can remove his outer coat? Who can approach him with a double bridle?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job41-14" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">14</span>    Who can open the doors of his face, With his terrible teeth all around?    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job41-15" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">15</span>    His rows of scales are his pride, Shut up tightly as with a seal;    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job41-16" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">16</span>    One is so near another That no air can come between them;    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job41-17" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">17</span>    They are joined one to another, They stick together and cannot be parted.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job41-18" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">18</span>    His sneezings flash forth light, And his eyes are like the eyelids of the morning.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job41-19" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">19</span>    Out of his mouth go burning lights; Sparks of fire shoot out.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job41-20" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">20</span>    Smoke goes out of his nostrils, As from a boiling pot and burning rushes.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job41-21" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">21</span>    His breath kindles coals, And a flame goes out of his mouth.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job41-22" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">22</span>    Strength dwells in his neck, And sorrow dances before him.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job41-23" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">23</span>    The folds of his flesh are joined together; They are firm on him and cannot be moved.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job41-24" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">24</span>    His heart is as hard as stone, Even as hard as the lower millstone.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job41-25" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">25</span>    When he raises himself up, the mighty are afraid; Because of his crashings they are beside <a href="http://draft.blogger.com/null" name="d"></a> themselves.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job41-26" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">26</span>    Though the sword reaches him, it cannot avail; Nor does spear, dart, or javelin.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job41-27" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">27</span>    He regards iron as straw, And bronze as rotten wood.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job41-28" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">28</span>    The arrow cannot make him flee; Slingstones become like stubble to him.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job41-29" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">29</span>    Darts are regarded as straw; He laughs at the threat of javelins.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job41-30" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">30</span>    His undersides are like sharp potsherds; He spreads pointed marks in the mire.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job41-31" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">31</span>    He makes the deep boil like a pot; He makes the sea like a pot of ointment.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job41-32" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">32</span>    He leaves a shining wake behind him; One would think the deep had white hair.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job41-33" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">33</span>    On earth there is nothing like him, Which is made without fear.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job41-34" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">34</span>    He beholds every high thing; He is king over all the children of pride."    </span><br /><br /><h4 style="text-align: center;">The End</h4><i>Forty Two</i><br /><span class="versetext" id="job42-7" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">7</span>    And so it was, after the Lord had spoken these words to Job, that  the Lord said to Eliphaz the Temanite, "My wrath is aroused against you  and your two friends, for you have not spoken of Me what is right, as My  servant Job has.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job42-8" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">8</span>    Now therefore, take for yourselves seven bulls and seven rams, go to  My servant Job, and offer up for yourselves a burnt offering; and My  servant Job shall pray for you. For I will accept him, lest I deal with  you according to your folly; because you have not spoken of Me what is  right, as My servant Job has."    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job42-9" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">9</span>    So Eliphaz the Temanite and Bildad the Shuhite and Zophar the  Naamathite went and did as the Lord commanded them; for the Lord had  accepted Job.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job42-10" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">&nbsp;</span></span><br /><br /><span class="versetext" id="job42-10" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">10</span>    And the Lord restored Job's losses <a href="http://draft.blogger.com/null" name="a"></a> when he prayed for his friends. Indeed the Lord gave Job twice as much as he had before.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job42-11" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">11</span>    Then all his brothers, all his sisters, and all those who had been  his acquaintances before, came to him and ate food with him in his  house; and they consoled him and comforted him for all the adversity  that the Lord had brought upon him. Each one gave him a piece of silver  and each a ring of gold.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job42-12" style="display: inline;"><span style="font-size: large;"><u><b><span class="versenum">12</span>    Now the Lord blessed the latter days of Job more than his beginning;  </b></u></span>for he had fourteen thousand sheep, six thousand camels, one thousand  yoke of oxen, and one thousand female donkeys.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job42-13" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">13</span>    He also had seven sons and three daughters.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job42-14" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">14</span>    And he called the name of the first Jemimah, the name of the second Keziah, and the name of the third Keren-Happuch.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job42-15" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">15</span>    In all the land were found no women so beautiful as the daughters of  Job; and their father gave them an inheritance among their brothers.&nbsp;</span><br /><br /><span class="versetext" id="job42-16" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">16</span>    After this Job lived one hundred and forty years, and saw his children and grandchildren for four generations.    </span>          <span class="versetext" id="job42-17" style="display: inline;"><span class="versenum">17</span>    So Job died, old and full of days.    </span><br /><br />
